metadata.title: Research HPV Vaccination to Cancer Elimination with Claude: Australia's Elimination Model
_variant_todo: rewrite every beat narration_text in the Teardown register (voices/teardown/VOICE.md + CLAUDE-BRAND.md) — voice only, facts unchanged
signature tangent 0-1 per video, ONLY on a clear opportunity (see SKILL)
swap the outro to the Claude outro from AUTHOR.MD :: NikBearBrown
then build audience-namespaced (audio in the new voice, scenes in the new palette)

skin_warnings: NO RENDERABLE BEATS: no beat carries a shot.remotion.pattern or Manim annotation — the pipeline will render NOTHING and every slot becomes a YOU-slate. The sheet was authored without the scene contract.
B00: palette=claude but the cold open is 'un-annotated' — COLD OPEN LAW wants ClaudeComposerAsk
B09: palette=claude but the outro is 'un-annotated' — OUTRO LAW wants ClaudeTitleOutro

beats.narration_text: Australia is on track to eliminate cervical cancer as a public health problem by 2028 — the first country to eliminate a cancer. The weapon is not a treatment. It is a vaccine.

build.suggested: suggested prompt: Australia is on track to eliminate cervical cancer as a public health problem by 2028 — the first country to eliminate a cancer. The weapon is not a treatment.

beats.narration_text: HPV vaccination above 80% coverage, combined with organized HPV-based primary screening, has dropped cervical cancer incidence in vaccinated Australian cohorts to near-zero. The WHO has set elimination at an incidence below 4 per 100,000 per year. Australia's 2028 projection rests on the cohort analysis: women vaccinated in 2007 at age 12-13 are now in their late 20s and early 30s, the peak incidence window, and their cervical cancer rates are near-zero. The model exists. The distribution problem is not a science problem.

build.suggested: suggested prompt: HPV vaccination above 80% coverage, combined with organized HPV-based primary screening, has dropped cervical cancer incidence in vaccinated Australian cohorts

beats.narration_text: Ask Claude to map the elimination timeline.

props.command: claude "Research Australia's cervical cancer elimination program. What are the HPV vaccination coverage rates achieved, what timeline of cervical cancer incidence decline has been documented in vaccinated cohorts, and when is elimination projected? Explain how the screening program shifted from cytology to HPV-based primary testing. Compare with the WHO 2030 global elimination strategy and which countries are on track vs. falling behind."

build.suggested: suggested prompt: Ask Claude to map the elimination timeline.

props.code:
# hpv_elimination_brief.md  --  Claude output

## Australia Elimination Model
- Coverage: >80% adolescent females
- Projection: ~2028 at <4/100,000 (Brotherton & Tabrizi 2020)
- Screening shift: Pap smear → HPV primary test (Dec 2017)
  Every 5 years from age 25 (was 2-year Pap from age 18)

## WHO 90-70-90 Targets
- 90% girls vaccinated by 15
- 70% screened with high-performance test
- 90% treated if pre-cancer or cancer detected
- 30+ countries achieved 90% vaccination coverage
- Only 8 low-income countries achieve 70% screening

## Countries Behind
- Sub-Saharan Africa, South Asia: coverage <30%
- Mozambique, Tanzania, Malawi: still primarily bivalent/no HPV access

## [UNVERIFIED] 2028 exact date — projections vary 2028-2035

build.suggested: suggested prompt: Read the elimination brief.

beats.narration_text: Elimination brief generated: Australia's greater than 80% vaccination coverage confirmed from AIHW data. 2028 elimination projection based on Brotherton and Tabrizi 2020 cohort modeling. Screening shift confirmed: Australia replaced Pap smear with HPV primary screening in December 2017 — now 5-year intervals from age 25. WHO 90-70-90 targets: currently 30-plus countries have achieved 90% vaccination coverage; only 8 low-income countries achieve 70% screening. Highest burden countries have less than 30% vaccination — still primarily bivalent or no HPV vaccine access. One unverified flag: 2028 exact elimination date — projections vary 2028-2035 depending on cohort definition.

build.suggested: suggested prompt: Elimination brief generated: Australia's greater than 80% vaccination coverage confirmed from AIHW data. 2028 elimination projection based on Brotherton and Tab

beats.narration_text: Ask Claude about global elimination barriers.

props.command: claude "Identify barriers preventing global HPV vaccination elimination and what infrastructure interventions have been shown to close coverage gaps — including cold chain, pricing, and political resistance."

build.suggested: suggested prompt: Ask Claude about global elimination barriers.

beats.narration_text: Global barrier analysis: Three structural barriers. First, cold chain — HPV vaccine requires refrigeration; single-dose schedule with Cecolin removes multi-dose logistics barrier for LMICs, with 1-dose efficacy confirmed in 2022. Second, cost — Gardasil 9 is $120-plus per dose in high-income markets; GAVI-negotiated price is under $5 per dose — but 50 countries are not GAVI-eligible and face middle-income country pricing gaps. Third, political resistance — Uganda's 2007 school-based delivery program dropped from 70% to under 10% following religious opposition. Evidence-based interventions that close gaps: school-based delivery programs — Rwanda achieved 93% coverage; community health worker implementation; male vaccination expansion removes gender-based hesitancy framing.

build.suggested: suggested prompt: Global barrier analysis: Three structural barriers. First, cold chain — HPV vaccine requires refrigeration; single-dose schedule with Cecolin removes multi-dose

beats.narration_text: Cervical cancer is a vaccine-preventable cancer whose primary burden falls on women in low-income countries with the least access to the vaccine. The elimination model exists and is proven. Australia's data is unambiguous. The distribution problem is not a science problem — it is an infrastructure, cost, and political problem. Each of those has a known intervention. Rwanda reached 93% coverage. The question is not whether elimination is possible but whether the political will exists to replicate it.
